strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance in long-horizon agent memory
Novel integration of truth-maintenance systems with LLM-based memory graphs
Explicit claim-level schema with validity intervals and contradiction edges
Proposes a concrete mechanism (TTMG) with detailed components
Reports efficiency gains (token reduction, latency improvement)
weaknesses: Results rely heavily on placeholder macros; no actual numbers are shown
Empirical evaluation is described but not presented; tables are empty
Ablation study lacks concrete data to support causal claims
No comparison to strong non-graph baselines (e.g., fine-tuned models)
Method complexity high; scalability to very long conversations unclear
required_revisions: Replace all placeholder result macros (e.g., \AmemLMEAcc, \TTMGLMEAcc) with actual numerical results
Fill Table 1, Table 2, Table 3, and Table 4 with complete experimental data
Provide ablation results (e.g., \AblContradict) with full numbers and statistical significance
Include cross-domain results (LoCoMo, MemoryAgentBench-CR) with actual metrics
Clarify how the 'maximum consistent subgraph' algorithm is implemented and its complexity
Add error bars or confidence intervals for reported accuracy/F1 scores

strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance for long-horizon agent memory.
Well-defined method: claim-level schema with temporal edges and consistency filtering.
Explicit ablation isolating the contribution of contradiction edges.
weaknesses: Critical results are placeholders (e.g., cross-domain, ablation values).
No statistical significance reported for claimed improvements.
Baseline comparison incomplete (Flat RAG results missing).
Ablation coverage is limited (only one component tested).
No analysis of failure modes or error distribution.

Evaluation limited to synthetic benchmarks; real-world scalability unclear
